entry.title: Re: Query Report Tips Part 1 - How to Display PO ME23N in SQ01
entry.content:
You don't even need to put in SU01. You can put the following code in the Code Section: DATA

DATA: zfield(1) TYPE c.
zfield = 'X'.
SET PARAMETER ID 'ENJ_CALL_ME23N' FIELD zfield.

Now add Transaction ME23 as Report Assignment in your report.

entry.title: Re: SAP Integration Suite, enhanced edition - The “Trust Layer” for Agentic AI
entry.content:
Hi Community,

"

1) Speed to value: AI that accelerates how integrations are built

Enhanced edition includes AI features aimed at boosting developer productivity, including:

  • Text‑to‑Flow generation — build integration flows using a natural language interface.
  • Script Optimization — analyze, refactor, and optimize Groovy or JavaScript scripts inside iFlows.

In an agentic world, integration teams must iterate faster — because agents will continuously evolve how they use tools, call APIs, and respond to events. These capabilities are about compressing the distance between an idea and a governed, deployable integration asset.

2) Operational trust: AI that helps integrations run with confidence

Agents introduce new scale and new patterns — and that means you need sharper operational insight. Enhanced edition includes AI-driven operations efficiency features such as:

  • API Anomaly Detection — AI-driven observability that detects unusual behavior and flags issues early.
  • API Traffic Prediction — identify trends and predict upcoming API call volumes to support smarter planning and load management.

This is what makes the positioning “agentic enabler” real: it’s not just that AI helps you build integrations — it helps you run the execution fabric with the resilience and foresight required for autonomous action.

"

 

Thanks for your selfless sharing again, it really help us a lot.

🙂

 

Regards,

ZFiori.

entry.title: Q1 2026 Innovations: SAP Enterprise Service Management
entry.content:
Register for our Q1 2026 Service Solutions webcast

2026 will prove to be a transformational year for many companies as AI assistants and agents drive high value in service delivery. I’m already excited about the Q2 Innovations blog! Get registered and ready for a preview at Sapphire 2026 of how SAP’s embedded AI innovations are delivering value for customers every day. But first things first, let me highlight some key innovations delivered in Q1 for SAP Enterprise Service Management (SAP ESM).

Ensuring seamless look and feel throughout a unified workspace

Delivering the right user experience has high impact on value and adoption for service specialists especially when the ESM workspace includes third party applications and processes. Using mashups is the preferred method to create an integrated environment ensuring service representatives don’t have to login to different systems or open sperate windows. Now the configuration for mashups has been extended to include theme parameters. With this setting the embedded mashup will adjust automatically to the system set theme providing a consistent look and feel for SAP and 3rd party embedded solutions.

Deliver connected experiences and activity across solutions

The event hub service in BTP allows solutions to publish inbound and outbound events ensuring others can access these activities for visibility and execution of line of business specific processes. SAP ESM now publishes outbound events to the SAP BTP Cloud Application Event Hub so other applications  receive them ensuring a unified and transparent experience across systems. This simplifies the need for system-to-system integration and centralizes events. This transparency can be realized with SAP SuccessFactors ensuring case updates are provided on employee service interactions directly on the SuccessFactors home page.

Self-Service available anytime, anywhere

Employees can get instant answers and create cases from the SAP SuccessFactors Mobile app providing easy 24/7 access for HR support. This provides greater flexibility and ease of access for HR related inquiries and gives employees the ability to work from their channel of choice, even if they are on the go. HR Case management is available as well, allowing creation of new cases, viewing and updating existing cases from wherever they are. Additionally, employees receive notifications when action is needed, ensuring case resolution without response delays.

Accelerating case resolution with UX simplicity

An approval process can delay case resolutions. Working with our customers’ inputs, several improvements have been made that reduce time, ensure consistency and enable a more streamlined handling of cases where approvals are needed. Ensuring quick steps to final resolution, both Service reps and managers benefit from a more efficient process and consistency while approvals are in progress. The following capabilities have been enabled to accelerate the approval process:

  • Approval steps enabled in auto flow scenarios
  • Case status triggers notifications or email
  • Directly approve from case header
  • Easy access to visibility in next steps and status
  • Read-only case state during approval steps


Flexing AI to accelerate case resolution

Another key improvement in Q1 includes extending the business information extraction capability to include custom fields. This capability saves time, reduces errors and delivers ease to service teams – eliminating the need to scour through email for relevant information and then having to make several manual entries. SAP to SAP solutions is rather logical, but often there are custom fields or 3rd party solutions. Now with custom fields included there’s additional flexibility ensuring service reps have improved automation, prepossessing and context to quickly close cases.

 
 

 

More Information & Thank you!

For additional Q1 innovations relevant for SAP Enterprise Service Management, reference Kirsi Tarvainen’s blog on SAP Service Cloud and register for the upcoming Q1 Release Webinar, and to keep up as we release capabilities each month reference the What’s New in our help portal. Please also reference these valuable links for more on SAP’s CX Customer Service Solutions:

SAP learning ESM Course

SAP Service Cloud V2 Certification Course

SAP Sales & Service Cloud Innovation Spotlight Webinar

To learn more about our Q1/2026 innovations across SAP’s CX solutions check out the blog!

Use the SAP Enterprise Service Management managed tag when posting on SAP ESM topics!

Finally, an enormous thank you to this expanding community, for your active participation and collaboration; as we all benefit – SAP employees, partners and customers -  from sharing our experiences and information on SAP ESM.

entry.title: Spring is Here, NYC SAP Community: Let's Meet Up! ☀️
entry.content:
Hey lovely community,

Can we just pause for a minute to admire this weather? After the lengthy New York winter, these warm spring days are really something else. I've been grabbing every chance to go to the park (to run of course :D) and just a leisurely afternoon with my dog and some SAP documentation on the laptop (yes, that's me, the guy). The park is always a big yes for me. 🌲



Which is why I'm actually reaching out: it's time for our next NYC SAP meetup, and I would like to get your thoughts.Here are a couple of simple questions for you👇

1. When do you prefer?

Are we a weeknight-after-work crowd, or do people like a weekend daytime vibe?
Specifically:Tuesday / Wednesday / Thursday evenings? Friday happy hour? Saturday afternoon in the park?

2. What time of day?

Early evening (6–8 PM) after work?
Later evening drinks (7:30 PM onward)? Afternoon / daytime on a weekend?

3. Indoor or outdoor where?

For me, with the weather being like this, I'd really fancy an outdoor activity: a park hangout, picnic-style, maybe Central Park like the first time, or somewhere in NJ because a lot of you are there.
However, if people around would rather a rooftop, a pub, or a coffee, let's hear it.

Comment below Let me know what suits you — day, time, and vibe.

According to what the majority says, I'll come up with a plan and we'll schedule a date. Can hardly wait to finally put faces to some of you (and seeing again those of you I haven't met for a while). Let's make it a great one.

See you soon ❤️ hopefully in a park! 🌳

entry.title: Re: MuleSoft vs SAP Integration Suite: Which One Fits Your Enterprise?
entry.content:
Thanks for sharing your perspective RubikWal. A few points I would like to share here - 

1. While SAP Integration Suite prioritizes a stateless architecture to ensure high performance and maintain 'Clean Core' principles, it does provides robust persistence capabilities through JMS Queues and Data Stores. 

2. In JMS Queues, in case of failures, we can see all the failed messages in the dead letter queue through in-built monitoring capability, plus we can select any of the failed message we want to reprocess and move it manually to process to backend.

3. Beyond JMS, SAP has AEM that takes it further, by offering Message Replay replay and enhanced visibility beyond standard JMS. 

4. To address the point on 'half-ready' error handling, SAP provides Standard Integration Flows for A2A and B2B scenarios that include out-of-the-box retry mechanisms. By leveraging Partner Directory , developers can simply configure connectivity for partners and applications rather than building from scratch. Furthermore, the platform offers the flexibility to customize error logic via Exception Sub-processes, ensuring that complex requirements are met.

entry.title: Re: Validation in 'Next Posting Date' in Tcode-F110
entry.content:
Some options are

1) Payment Proposal Review:
Ensure that the proposal run is checked before the payment run. The proposal will show invoices with future posting dates, and you can exclude them manually.

2) Payment Run Parameters:
In F110, the "Posting Date for Payment Document" is set during the run. If this is earlier than the invoice posting date, SAP still allows it. You can restrict the "Next Posting Date" so that it doesn’t extend beyond the current period. This is a configuration setting in the payment run parameters.

3) Payment Method Configuration (FBZP):
Few clients handle this by restricting payment methods to only pick invoices that are due within the current posting period. In FBZP, under payment method settings, you can control "Earliest Due Date" logic.

In short, without enhancements, the most effective control is to tighten F110 parameters and payment block usage. That way, invoices dated in April won’t be picked up in a March run.

entry.title: Re: How to prevent clearing Vendor Payment with another V...
entry.content: SAP does not allow clearing invoices of one vendor against another in standard functionality. If you are seeing this, it is either a master data setup (head office/branch, alternate payee) or user selection issue. To prevent it, review vendor master relationships and tighten clearing parameters/authorizations.

entry.title: SAP AI CoreをClineと連携してVibe Codingしてみよう!
entry.content:
はじめに

近年、生成AIの急速な進化により、ソフトウェア開発の現場は大きな転換期を迎えています。GitHub Copilot や Cursor に代表されるAIコーディングアシスタントが普及する中、タスクを自律的に計画・実行できるエージェント型AIツールへと進化が進んでいます。Clineはその代表格であり、VS Code上で AI が第二の開発者（いや、第一？）として機能します。いわゆる Vibe Coding というやつです。

Cline では API Provider （Google や Open AI, Anthropic 等）を選択して生成AIモデルを利用しますが、その API Provider として SAP AI Core を選択することができます。本ブログでは、SAP AI Core を Cline と連携し、Generative AI Hub にデプロイされた生成AIモデルを利用する方法をご紹介します。

 

前提条件

本ブログは、以下の内容を前提条件として構成しています。

  • SAP AI CoreのサービスをBTP Cloud Foundry環境で有効化済み
  • VS Codeをインストール済み

ローカルのシステム情報

本ブログは、以下のシステム上で動作を確認しています。

  • OS -- macOS Tahoe 26.3.1
  • VS Code -- Version: 1.109.5

 

設定手順

1. VS Code の拡張機能「Cline」をインストール

VS Codeを開きます。

1. 左側のパネルから "Extensions" を押下します。

2. 検索バーにて "cline" を検索します。

3. "Cline" を開きます。

4. "Install" を押下して、拡張機能「Cline」をインストールします。

2. Cline の設定画面を開く

Clineのインストールが完了すると、左側のパネルに Cline のアイコンが表示されます。

1. アイコンをクリックして、Cline の画面を開きます。

2. 右上の設定アイコンから Settings 画面を開きます。

3. API Provider で "SAP AI Core" 選択します。

3. SAP AI Core の Client Credentials を取得する

Cline の API Configuration タブから API Provider に SAP AI Core を選択すると、SAP AI Core のクレデンシャルが求められます。

BTP Cockpitへアクセスし、Client Credentials を取得します。

1. BTP Cockpit - Subaccount の Services > Service Marketplace から SAP AI Core のサービスを作成します。

 

2. 下記項目を選択、入力して SAP AI Core のインスタンスを作成します。

  • Service: SAP AI Core
  • Plan: extended
    ※ LLMへのアクセスが必要なため、extended planが必須です。
  • Runtime Environments: Cloud Foundry
  • Space: <任意のスペース>
  • Instance Name: <任意のインスタンス名>

 

3. 2で作成した、SAP AI Core のインスタンスのビューページへアクセスし、サービスキーを作成します。

 

4. 下記項目を入力してサービスキーを作成します。

  • Service Key Name: <任意のサービスキー名>

 

5. 4で作成したサービスキー名を押下し、発行されたサービスキーを表示します。

 

6. VS Code の Cline > API Configuration にサービスキーの Client Credentials を入力します。

Cline > API ConfigurationSAP AI Core > Service key
AI Core Client Idclient id
AI Core Client Secretclientsecret
AI Core Base URLserviceurls/AI_API_URL
AI Core Auth URLurl
AI Core Resource Group"default"

 

4. Clineを使用する

「3. SAP AI Core の Client Credentials を取得する」ステップが完了すると、Generative AI Hub にデプロイされている生成AIモデルが読み込まれます。"Model" プルダウンから使用するモデルを選択し、設定を完了します。

 

ここまでで Cline で SAP AI Core を使うための設定が終了しました。

それでは実際に Cline を使ってみましょう！

今回は例として、以下のプロンプトを実行します。

  • "Hello world !!!"を標準出力するプログラムをpythonで実装して

 

すぐにPythonでの実装が確認できました。

 

終わりに

本ブログでは、SAP AI Core を Cline から使用する方法についてご紹介しました。

SAP AI Core の Client Credentials を Cline 側で設定することで、Generative AI Hub にデプロイされている生成AIモデルが利用できます。

みなさんも Cline を活用して、Vibe Coding Life を楽しみましょう！

entry.title: Re: How to handle multiple cost categories per PO line in...
entry.content:
No, the standard Reconcile GR/IR Accounts Fiori app (F3302) in SAP S/4HANA does not support splitting a single PO line into multiple analytical items by cost category. The app reconciles at the PO line level. So freight, customs, and material bundled into one line will always appear together. To achieve category-level separation, you need either PO design changes (separate line items per category) or custom enhancements.

Below are some options to achieve Category-Level separation

1) Best Practice - Separate PO Lines
Create distinct PO lines for material, freight, customs, etc. Each line then appears separately in GR/IR reconciliation. This is the cleanest way to align with different workflows, aging logic, and responsible teams.

2) Account Assignment Split
Use different account assignment categories or valuation classes for each cost component. This allows reporting separation in FI/CO, though the GR/IR app still shows one PO line.

3) Enhancement Options
Custom CDS views: Extend the GR/IR reconciliation app with a custom analytical view that splits by cost category field (if available in PO or invoice data).
BAdIs / Enhancements: Implement logic to derive and display subcategories in a custom Fiori app or extension of F3302.
Workflow routing: Use workflow rules based on account assignment or G/L account to direct items to different teams, even if they appear as one line in the app.

entry.title: Time Valuation for 4 Cycle Shifts | SAP SuccessFactors Time Tracking
entry.content:
Hello,

We have a requirement for Ireland involving a 4‑cycle clock-time–based work schedule (for example, 4 Cycle A), which spans 28 days across 4 weeks. In this schedule:

  • Week 1: Employees work 60 hours
  • Week 2: Employees work 24 hours
  • …and the pattern continues for the remaining weeks.

    From the 60 hours worked in Week 1:

    • 40 hours are paid as Basic hrs
    • The remaining 20 hours are split as follows:
      • 2 hours are paid as Overtime @ 1.5
      • 16 hours are carried forward to Week 2 and added to its 24 Basic hours, totaling 40 Basic hours
      • The remaining 2 hours (from the original 20) are paid as Overtime @ 1.5 in Week 2

This carryover logic continues for the following weeks in the cycle.

Question:
How can I retrieve the number of hours worked from the previous week in the Time Valuation so that these hours can be used for the Basic pay and Overtime calculation in the current week?

Thanks,
Mohd Saif Ansari

4 Cycle A Work Schedule:

entry.title: How to Display Position Code in Latest People Profile Header – SAP SuccessFactors EC Workaround
entry.content:
In the legacy People Profile of SAP SuccessFactors, the position code was displayed alongside the position name, making it easy for consultants and HR users to identify positions quickly.

However, in the latest People Profile (introduced after last upgrades), only the position name is displayed. The position code is no longer visible in the profile header, making it time-consuming for users to retrieve this information from the Position Org Chart.

To address this limitation, I explored a workaround solution that enables displaying the position code again in the latest People Profile header.

Legacy people profile – Earlier position code is available beside of the position name


Latest people profile- Only the position name is displayed, and the position code is no longer visible in the header.

Workaround Solution:
Step 1: Go to configure latest people profile > General settings


Step 2: Click on configure header fields

Step 3: Select first employee information type as Position

If Position Field Is Not Available in Dropdown - It needs to be synced to a UDF using HRIS Sync Mapping., For mapping Position field the UDF please follow the below steps.
3.1. Go to the Manage Business Configuration > Job Information > Position Field > Click on details

3.2. Add an HRIS Sync Mapping for the Position field. Map it to an unused standard element field ; for example, Customizable Field 7 can be used for this purpose. Once the mapping is done, click on Save.

3.3. Go to HRIS sync mapping check your added field weather is appearing or not.

3.4. Once HRIS Sync job will run the field data automatically available in the UDF. After that you can use this created Customizable field 7 “Position” in configure header fields.

Step 4: Save the general settings

Step 5: Navigate to the Employee Profile and Verify that the Position Code is now displayed along with the Position Name in the header

Conclusion

This workaround restores the visibility of the position code in the latest People Profile header by leveraging header field configuration and HRIS Sync Mapping. As a result, HR users and consultants can quickly identify positions without navigating to the Position Org Chart, improving efficiency and overall user experience.

entry.title: Re: only active database instances are loaded into system...
entry.content:
Hi Team, 

Memory will be allocated to Hana DB, based on the configuration parameter "global.ini -> [memorymanager] -> global_allocation_limit" .

If you want to limit the memory of a Tenant DB or any service , Please configure the parameter :<service>.ini -> [memorymanager] -> allocationlimit

Refer more details in the KBA 1999997 - FAQ: SAP HANA Memory

Hope this is helpful.

~Gaurav

entry.title: OData $batch Deleting Records Across Multiple Tables in Single Transaction
entry.content:
Introduction

 

Instead of sending each operation individually, OData batch processing enables you to transmit several operations (such as create, update, or delete) in a single request. This implies that you can use a single HTTP call to the $batch endpoint in SAP Gateway to execute actions on several tables at once.

For instance, you can make a single request to remove both a header record and the item records that are associated with it. When several records need to be managed as a single unit, this is helpful.

Batch processing enhances performance by minimizing network calls and ensures data consistency through change sets, which execute all operations as a single transactional unit. In case of any failure, the entire transaction is rolled back, thereby preventing partial data modifications.

Business Scenario

In the Manage Supplier Master application, a supplier’s complete data is not stored in a single table. Instead, it is distributed across multiple tables:

  • Basic Data (Supplier Name, Type)
  • Address Data
  • Identification Data (PAN, GST, etc.)
  • Purchasing Organization Data

Problem Statement

In the Manage Supplier Master application, when a user deletes a supplier, the system must remove all associated records from multiple backend tables, including basic details, address information, identification records, and purchasing organization data.

However, handling these deletions individually leads to several challenges:

  • Multiple HTTP calls are triggered for each table deletion
  • Increased network latency and poor performance
  • Risk of partial deletion (e.g., one table deleted, others not)
  • Data inconsistency across the system.

Prerequisites:

Before you implement batch delete in OData, make sure the following things are ready:

1. OData(V2) Service Setup: 

You should already have an SAP Gateway OData (v2) service created and registered.
All required tables (like ZTable1 , ZTable2) must be exposed as entity sets and should support DELETE operation.
Also, make sure runtime objects (Data Provider Class and Model) are generated using SEGW or CDS.

2. Entity Set Configuration:

Each table should be defined as an entity set with a key field.
In the service metadata (EDMX), batch processing ($batch) is available by default in OData v2.

3. Methods Implementation:

In the Data Provider Class:

  • Implement DELETE_ENTITY method for each entity (table).
  • Also implement:
    • CHANGESET_BEGIN
    • CHANGESET_PROCESS
    • CHANGESET_END

These help handle batch operations properly.

By default, only one delete is allowed per change set.
If you want multiple deletes in one go (same transaction), you need to enable deferred mode .

  METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~changeset_begin.
    cv_defer_mode = abap_true.
  ENDMETHOD.

Why do we use deferred mode in OData batch processing?

Normal Behavior (Without Deferred Mode)

By default in SAP Gateway:

  • Each operation in a change set is handled individually
  • Only one DELETE/UPDATE/CREATE is allowed per change set
  • Each operation is processed immediately

So we cannot group multiple deletes in one transaction

With Deferred Mode

When you enable deferred mode:

  • All operations in the change set are collected first
  • Then they are processed together in CHANGESET_PROCESS
  • You can handle multiple deletes/updates in one go
  • Deferred mode ensures that all operations in a batch are executed sequentially in a controlled manner without interruption, as part of a single transaction.

This allows you to treat them as one logical unit (single LUW)

Implementation

Step 1:   Creating Database table

Zdt_mini_akbasic( basic information table for the supplier) 

Zdt_mini_ak_add( address table for the supplier) 

Zdt_mini_a_ident( Identity table for the Supplier )

Zdt_mini_a_porg( Purchase organization table )

Step 2:  Now need to create the OData project with all the tables/Structure

METHOD /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_srv_runtime~changeset_process.

  DATA: ls_changeset_request  TYPE /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_types=>ty_s_changeset_request,
        ls_changeset_response TYPE /iwbep/if_mgw_appl_types=>ty_s_changeset_response,
        lv_supplier_no        TYPE zde_mini_snumber,
        lv_request_line       TYPE string,
        ls_header             TYPE /iwbep/s_mgw_name_value_pair,
        lo_busi_exception     TYPE REF TO /iwbep/cx_mgw_busi_exception,
        lv_msg                TYPE string.

  LOOP AT it_changeset_request INTO ls_changeset_request.

    TRY.
        IF ls_changeset_request-operation_type = text-001.

          " Read each request header (name/value) and look for DELETE line
          LOOP AT ls_changeset_request-request_headers INTO ls_header.
             lv_request_line = |{ls_header-name} {ls_header-value}|.

            " Try to extract SupplierNumber from this line
            FIND PCRE 'SuppilerNumber=''([^'']+)''' " New syntax for Regular Expression
                 IN lv_request_line
                 SUBMATCHES lv_supplier_no.
            IF lv_supplier_no IS NOT INITIAL.
              EXIT. " Stop searching headers once we found the key
            ENDIF.
          ENDLOOP.

          IF lv_supplier_no IS INITIAL.
            MESSAGE e000(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception = NEW /iwbep/cx_mgw_busi_exception( ).
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
              iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
              iv_msg_text =  CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Basic Data
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_akbasic
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e002(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Address
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_ak_add
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e003(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Identification
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_a_ident
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e004(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

          " Delete from Purchasing Org
          IF sy-subrc = 0.
            DELETE FROM zdt_mini_a_porg
              WHERE suppiler_number = lv_supplier_no.
          ELSE.
            MESSAGE e005(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
            lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
            iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
            iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
            ).
            RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
          ENDIF.

        ENDIF.

        " Maintain operation number in the response
        ls_changeset_response-operation_no = ls_changeset_request-operation_no.
        APPEND ls_changeset_response TO ct_changeset_response.
      CATCH cx_root INTO DATA(lx_root).
        MESSAGE e000(zakmini) INTO lv_msg.
        lo_busi_exception->get_msg_container( )->add_message_text_only(
        iv_msg_type = zif_ak_constant=>c_msgty_error
        iv_msg_text = CONV bapi_msg( lv_msg )
        ).
        RAISE EXCEPTION lo_busi_exception.
    ENDTRY.

    CLEAR: ls_changeset_request, ls_changeset_response, lv_supplier_no, lv_request_line.

  ENDLOOP.

ENDMETHOD.
  method /IWBEP/IF_MGW_APPL_SRV_RUNTIME~CHANGESET_END.
    COMMIT WORK.
  endmethod.

What COMMIT WORK Actually Does

  • Saves all successful operations to database
  • Ends the LUW (Logical Unit of Work)
  • Makes changes visible to other users.

Output:

--batch
Content-Type: multipart/mixed; boundary=changeset


--changeset
Content-Type: application/http
Content-Transfer-Encoding: binary


DELETE zakBasicSet(SupplierNumber='S0038') HTTP/1.1


--changeset--
--batch--

Conclusion:

  1. Once the $batch request is received, SAP Gateway identifies the changeset and starts processing it as a single transaction (LUW).
  2. The framework then reads each operation inside the changeset and calls the respective method.
  3. All operations inside the changeset are executed sequentially (one by one) but within the same transaction context.
  4. The backend logic performs all related table deletions, but no database commit happens immediately during execution.
  5. After all operations are successfully completed, COMMIT WORK is triggered automatically; if any operation fails, the entire changeset is rolled back, ensuring no partial data is saved.

entry.title: Exploring AI-Driven Finance Use Cases: Seeking Inputs from Experts
entry.content:
In today’s rapidly evolving business landscape, Finance functions are undergoing a major transformation driven by data, automation, and Artificial Intelligence (AI). As part of the CIS Innovation Incubation team, we are working on building AI-driven use cases to enhance efficiency, improve decision-making, and deliver value to customers and partners.

Why Finance?
Finance plays a critical role in every organization, covering areas such as financial planning, accounts management, risk, compliance, and forecasting. However, many processes still involve manual effort, disconnected systems, and delayed insights.

Our Objective
We are focusing on:

  • Identifying real-world pain points in Finance processes
  • Understanding customer and partner challenges
  • Co-creating impactful AI-driven solutions

Potential Areas of Innovation

  • Automated anomaly detection in financial data
  • Intelligent forecasting using predictive analytics
  • AI-based document processing for invoices and records
  • Conversational assistants for quick insights
  • Real-time risk and compliance monitoring

Collaboration Approach

  • Engaging with Product Managers and Finance teams
  • Conducting discussions to gather inputs and pain points
  • Running initial development in parallel for quick prototyping

Call for Inputs
We would like to hear from the community:

  • What are the key challenges you face in Finance today?
  • Which processes are time-consuming or error-prone?
  • Where do you see the highest potential for AI adoption?

Your inputs will help us build meaningful and scalable solutions.

Next Steps
Based on the feedback, we will prioritize use cases, develop prototypes, and iterate with stakeholders.

Conclusion
AI has the potential to transform Finance from reactive reporting to proactive decision-making. Collaboration will be key to achieving this transformation.

Looking forward to your inputs.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA: “Track Sales Order” app is showing incorrect...
entry.content:
Good day @Rajender123 

Thank you for your question.

The standard Public Cloud behaviour, you cannot simply force the status in Track Sales Orders or Manage Sales Order to Completely Processed just because the other is cancelled or because some items are rejected. Rejected items can still leave the order header as Open, In Process, or even Not Relevant, depending on the item category and on how the rejection reason is configured.

Here are references for more guiding details.

3136916 - Rejected sales order items remain open - SAP for Me

3085049 - The overall status of a sales document is blank or 'Not Relevant' while all items have been rejected - SAP for Me

3568639 - Sales Order Remaining in "Open" Status after Rejecting all items in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

So, you cannot always mark a sales order as completely processed. A fully rejected order does not automatically become “Completely Processed” in every case.

In a situation where some items are rejected and the remaining items are complete; the order should only become completely processed if th rejected items are truly closed from a status perspective. The key check is the rejection reason configuration in SSCUI 102494, especially the No Billing flag. If that flag is not aligned with your process, the rejected item can continue to keep the order from becoming fully completed.

See the following references for more insights.

3102794 - Rejected sales documents listed in the "Create Billing Documents" app in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

3356298 - Cancelled sales order item with status open - SAP for Me

The most likely reason you cannot resolve your scenario with KBA 3136916 is not only about the rejection reason itself, but also about the item category logic and the way the status is derived in the app. This depends on the combination of item delivery relevance, billing relevance, and rejection setup.

Your expectation that a cancelled order should show a clearer closed status is understandable, but from the SAP standard logic, “rejected” and “completed” are not always the same thing. In these apps, the displayed fulfillment or overall processing status is obtained from logistics and billing status logic, not only from the business meaning of cancellation.

I would recommend you:

  1. Use CBC for SSCUI 102494 and check the exact rejection reason used on the affected sales order items and confirm whether No Billing is set correctly. See KBA 3136916, 3102794 and 3356298 for more details.
  2. Check whether the affected item category has special status. The reason is, if the affected sales order items use an item category with special delivery or billing relevance logic, the header may stay open even after rejection. See KBA 3568639 for some insights.
  3. Confirm the status behaviour in Manage Sales Orders or Manage Sales Orders. If the backend sales order header and item statuses are already standard there, then Track Sales Orders is only reflecting that standard logic rather than introducing a separate error.

In Summary:

  • The behaviour is generally standard and not necessarily a system defect.
  • A cancelled or rejected sales order does not automatically move to Completely Processed.
  • The overall fulfillment status comes from item level delivery and billing status logic.
  • You should check SSCUI 102494, especially the No Billing indicator.
  • The next step is to test the scenario in Manage Sales Orders

  and Track SO Fulfillment to confirm whether the result is caused by rejection reason setup, item category logic, or remaining billing relevance.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

SAP runs SAP

It’s more than a slogan—it’s a commitment. SAP’s own IT organization (CPIT) acts as Customer Zero, adopting and validating SAP innovations before they reach the market. This means every new capability, every architectural shift, and every AI breakthrough is tested first inside one of the world’s most complex enterprise landscapes.

In 2026, we’re taking you behind the scenes again with a new season of Inside SAP IT, our live YouTube series where SAP experts share unfiltered insights, lessons learned, and the real stories behind SAP’s internal transformation.

Whether you’re an IT leader, architect, developer, or business transformation professional, this series gives you a front‑row seat to how SAP builds, scales, and operates the technologies shaping the intelligent, agentic enterprise.

entry.title: Re: SAP BW Bridge or Data Product Generator (DPG): What would you choose
entry.content:
Hello Ankit,

I am sure you don't necessarily need BW Bridge to connect an S/4 on premise ODP extractor to the Datasphere. I am partly still using old extractors (like 0FI_AR_4, because SAP has not yet provided a CDS extractor for this). We are running a cloud connector service and the DP Agent for all purposes where we can't load data via CDS, without using BW Bridge.

Of course you still need a BW Bridge if your logic is on BW side.

Kind regards Jochen

entry.title: SAP ASE - Rolling SPS UPGRADE in HADR System
entry.content:
The HADR feature allows SAP ASE applications to operate with zero downtime while you are updating the SAP ASE software.

     Generally, the steps for performing a rolling upgrade are:

  • Upgrade the standby components
  • Perform a failover
  • Upgrade the components that are on standby after the failover

Complete the upgrade steps in a single sequence: partial upgrade is not supported (for example, you cannot upgrade some components now and then upgrade the other components at another time). Replication is suspended during some steps of a rolling upgrade, and if you perform a partial upgrade, logs continue to grow, which can result in logs or the SPQ running out of space. In the course of a rolling upgrade, it is not required for the SAP ASE and Replication Server versions to be identical.

In this blog we upgraded the SAP ASE version from 16.0 SP03 PL09 to 16.0 SP04 PL06. Also Let’s assume that we have following sites names for Primary, Secondary and DR Location.

Primary – Site1                                  Secondary – Site2                              DR – Site3

We have few prerequisites below which we need to consider before starting the upgrade.

isql -Usa -P <password> -S<hostname>:<port> -X -w999
sp_version
go

sp_configure “enable java”
go

sp_configure “auditing”
go

sp_lmconfig

Repeat the steps for Secondary and DR and collect the screenshots.

Log into the DR node's RMA as the user DR admin using isql , and issue below command to start the upgrade process and then shut down RMA

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMA Port> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, start, Site3(DR)

shutdown
go

Install the new version of Replication Server using the setup.bin utility. Go to download directory extract the software using SAPCAR and run setup.bin

Check if process is running

 

Exit the installer and start RMA
Go to below location and run the command ./rma & to start the RMA
Location- /Sybase/<SID>/ASE-16_0/bin

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, finish, Site3(DR)

 
Post upgrade check replication status
sap status path

Upgrade the SAP ASE database at DR Node
isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, start, site3

Shutdown backup Server

isql_r64 -Usa -P<Password> -S<SID> -x -w999
shutdown SYB_ BACKUP
go 


Shutdown ASE Server
shutdown
go 

Install the new version of ASE Server using the setup.bin utility. Go to download directory extract the software using SAPCAR and run setup.bin


After the SAP installer has completed the upgrade, use the updatease utility to upgrade SAP ASE, which runs installmaster and performs other tasks to bring SAP ASE up to date. updatease is available as a GUI display or a command line tool.


Enter the Server name <SID> and password <sa user> 

Finished the upgrade the SAP ASE database at DR Node
isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, finish, site3

Stop the Fault Manager before you perform a rolling upgrade (even if you are performing planned activities like a planned failover). You can start the Fault Manager after the upgrade is complete. To stop the Fault Manager, issue this from the <installation_directory>/FaultManager directory:


<Fault_Manager_install_dir>/FaultManager/bin/sybdbfm stop


Log into the Primary node RMA as the user DR_admin using isql , and issue below command to start the upgrade process and then shutdown RMA

isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<port> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, start, Site1(Primary)


shutdown
go


check RMA process: 
ps -ef |grep repserver 
ps -ef |grep rma


Once Sybase Replication Server (SRS) upgrade finished exit the installer and start RMA
Go to below location and run the command ./rma & to start the RMA
Location- /Sybase/<SID>/ASE-16_0/bin

Finish the SRS upgrade with below command.

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<port> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, finish, Site1(Primary)


Perform Failover from Site1 (Primary) to Site2 (Secondary) – Log into RMA on site1 as the DR_admin user and issue:

sap_failover <site1 >, <site2 >, 30

This command allows a 30-second grace period for any running transactions to complete before the deactivation starts. Failover will not succeed if there are still active transactions after 30 seconds. If this occurs, retry the command when the system is not busy, use a longer grace period, or use the force option to terminate the client connection (if it is safe) with:

sap_failover <site1 >, <site2 >, 30, force

The sap_failover command may take a long time to finish. To check the status of the sap_failover command, issue this from the RMA:
sap_status task

 

Use the sap_failover_drain_to_dr command while performing failover inside an HADR with DR node environment. The sap_failover_drain_to_dr command makes sure that the incremental backlog from the HA cluster is drained to the DR node.

sap_failover_drain_to_dr 30

sap_status task

Once the sap_status command returns Completed, resume replication by issuing this from the RMA:
sap_host_available <site1> (Primary)
sap_host_available <site3> (DR)

Log into the Secondary node’s RMA as the user DR admin using isql, and issue below command to start the upgrade process and then shutdown RMA

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, start, Site2(Secondary)

shutdown
go


check RMA process: 
ps -ef |grep repserver 
ps -ef |grep rma


Once Sybase Replication Server (SRS) upgrade finished exit the installer and start RMA
Go to below location and run the command ./rma & to start the RMA
Location- /Sybase/<SID>/ASE-16_0/bin

Finish the SRS upgrade with below command.

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<port> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server SRS, finish, Site2(Secondary)

Upgrade the Primary node's (SITE1) SAP ASE database.

isql -U DR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, start, site1(Primary)

isql_r64 -Usa -P<Password> -S<SID> -x -w999
shutdown SYB_ BACKUP
go
 
Shutdown ASE Server

shutdown
go


Install the new version of ASE Server using the setup.bin utility. Go to download directory extract the software using SAPCAR and run setup.bin
Run the updatease utility to upgrade SAP ASE,  Enter the Server name <SID> and password <sa user>

Finished the upgrade the SAP ASE database at Site1 Node

isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, finish, site1 (Primary)

Failback to Site1 – Login on current Primary Database (Site2)
isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_failover <site2 >, <site1 >, 30

sap_status task
sap_failover_drain_to_dr 30
sap_status task
sap_host_available <site2> (Secondary)
sap_host_available <site3> (DR)


Upgrade the Secondary node's (SITE2) SAP ASE database.

isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, start, site2(Secondary)

isql_r64 -Usa -P<Password> -S<SID> -x -w999
shutdown SYB_ BACKUP
go
 
Shutdown ASE Server

shutdown
go


Install the new version of ASE Server using the setup.bin utility. Go to download directory extract the software using SAPCAR and run setup.bin
Run the updatease utility to upgrade SAP ASE, Enter the Server name <SID> and password <sa user>

Finished the upgrade the SAP ASE database at Site1 Node

isql -UDR_admin -P <password> -S<hostname>:<RMAPort> -X -w999
sap_upgrade_server ASE, finish, site2 (Secondary)

This blog help us to understand an approach for upgrade sap ASE (SYBASE) in HADR environment

entry.title: CodeSage for SAP - (Building an AI-Powered ABAP Knowledge Platform from Scratch)
entry.content:
What is CodeSage for SAP:

The most prominent technical use of "CodeSage" is for a series of open-source embedding models developed for software engineering tasks like code search and representation learning. Using the same concept wants to create a code knowledge assistant for ABAP development teams. It gives every developer — new or experienced — the ability to query your organization's entire codebase in plain English and receive accurate, contextualized answers grounded in your own source code.

Who will build the CodeSage for SAP:

This artifact will be very useful for multiple purposes, such as learning and project delivery etc. It could be built as a project by institutions, large orgs running on SAP.  This is trained on specific ABAP landscape: naming conventions, Codes, error classes, enhancement patterns, clean core approach, SAP best practice etc.

How to build CodeSage for SAP:

Phase

What It Builds

Runs Where

Phase 1

Extracts ABAP source from SAP via RFC

Windows PC

Phase 2

Embeds code into ChromaDB vector index

Windows PC (CPU)

Phase 3

Fine-tunes LLaMA-3 with QLoRA, deploys to BYOM

Kaggle (free GPU)

Phase 4

Deploys CAP agent on BTP for developer queries

SAP BTP

 PHASE 1  —  Scan & Extract

Phase 1 reads your SAP system and saves every custom ABAP object (Z*/Y* namespace) as plain text files on your local Windows machine. The output is a folder of .abap source files and .json metadata sidecars that feed both Phase 2 and Phase 3.

Think of it as taking a complete photograph of your codebase at a point in time. The extractor uses pyrfc — a Python library that speaks SAP's RFC protocol — to call standard SAP function modules (RPY_PROGRAM_READ, RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ) and pull source code line by line. A credential filter strips any hard-coded passwords before files are written to disk.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

scan_config.yaml — controls what gets extracted

sap:
  host:     "your-sap-host"
  sysnr:    "00"
  client:   "100"
  user:     "CODESAGE_RFC"
  password: "${SAP_RFC_PASSWORD}"    # Never store passwords in plain text

output_dir: "./abap_files"

namespaces:
  - "Z*"
  - "Y*"

object_types:
  - PROG    # Programs / Reports
  - FUGR    # Function Groups (contains Function Modules)
  - CLAS    # ABAP Classes
  - INTF    # Interfaces
  - ENHO    # BAdI Implementations

exclude_packages:
  - "$TMP"  # Skip local dev objects

extractor.py — RFC extraction script

import pyrfc, json, pathlib, yaml, re

cfg = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
out = pathlib.Path(cfg['output_dir'])
out.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# Strip any hard-coded credentials before saving to disk
CRED_RE = re.compile(
r'(password|passwd|pwd|secret|apikey)\s*=\s*[\'\"][^\'\"]+[\'\"]', re.I)

conn = pyrfc.Connection(
ashost=cfg['sap']['host'], sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
passwd=cfg['sap']['password'])

def save_object(name, obj_type, source, meta):
source_clean = CRED_RE.sub('[REDACTED]', source)
(out / f'{name}.abap').write_text(source_clean, encoding='utf-8')
(out / f'{name}.json').write_text(json.dumps(meta, indent=2), encoding='utf-8')

# Extract Programs
progs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='PROG', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in progs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_PROGRAM_READ', PROG_NAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r['SOURCE'])
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'PROG', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'PROG',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

# Extract Function Modules
fugs = conn.call('RPY_DIRECTORY_FINISH', OBJECT_TYPE='FUGR', GENERIC_NAME='Z*')
for obj in fugs['TADIR']:
try:
r = conn.call('RPY_FUNCTIONMODULE_READ', FUNCNAME=obj['OBJ_NAME'])
src='\n'.join(l['LINE'] for l in r.get('SOURCE',[]))
save_object(obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'FUGR', src, {
'name': obj['OBJ_NAME'], 'type': 'FM',
'package': obj.get('DEVCLASS',''), 'changed': obj.get('LDATE','')})
except Exception as e:
print(f' SKIP {obj["OBJ_NAME"]}: {e}')

print(f'Done. {len(list(out.glob("*.abap")))} objects saved to {out}/')

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step 1 — Install SAP NW RFC SDK

# 1. Download "SAP NW RFC SDK 7.50 Windows 64-bit" from SAP Support Portal (Note 2573790)
# 2. Extract to C:\nwrfcsdk\  (you should see C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll)
# 3. Add to Windows PATH:
#    Win+S -> "Edit the system environment variables" -> Environment Variables
#    -> System Variables -> Path -> Edit -> New -> C:\nwrfcsdk\lib -> OK
# 4. Verify in a NEW PowerShell window:
Get-Item "C:\nwrfcsdk\lib\sapnwrfc.dll"   # Should list the file

Step 2 — Create virtual environment and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract

python -m venv codesage-env
.\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1
# If blocked by execution policy, run this first:
Set-ExecutionPolicy -ExecutionPolicy RemoteSigned -Scope CurrentUser

pip install pyrfc pyyaml
python -c "import pyrfc; print('pyrfc OK')"
python -c "import yaml;  print('pyyaml OK')"

Step 3 — Store SAP password as environment variable

# Current session only (recommended for dev/test):
$env:SAP_RFC_PASSWORD = "your_sap_password_here"

# Persist across reboots (stored in Windows registry):
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
    "SAP_RFC_PASSWORD", "your_sap_password_here", "User")

Step 4 — Test RFC connection

# Save as test_connection.py, then run: python test_connection.py
import pyrfc, os, yaml
cfg  = yaml.safe_load(open('scan_config.yaml'))
conn = pyrfc.Connection(
    ashost=cfg['sap']['host'],   sysnr=cfg['sap']['sysnr'],
    client=cfg['sap']['client'], user=cfg['sap']['user'],
    passwd=os.environ['SAP_RFC_PASSWORD'])
result = conn.call('RFC_PING')
print('RFC connection successful:', result)
# Expected:  RFC connection successful: {}

python test_connection.py

Step 5 — Run the extractor

cd C:\codesage\phase1_extract
python extractor.py
# Console shows: SKIP messages for deleted/inaccessible objects (normal)
# Finishes with: Done. 8,432 objects saved to .\abap_files\

Step 6 — Verify output

# Count extracted files
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\abap_files" -Filter "*.abap").Count
# Typical: 5,000 to 20,000

# Confirm no plain-text passwords in output
Select-String -Path ".\abap_files\*.abap" -Pattern "password" -CaseSensitive:$false |
  Where-Object { $_.Line -notmatch "REDACTED" }
# If nothing returned: credential filter is working correctly

Step 7 — Schedule monthly re-run (optional)

$action  = New-ScheduledTaskAction `
    -Execute "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\python.exe" `
    -Argument "C:\codesage\phase1_extract\extractor.py" `
    -WorkingDirectory "C:\codesage\phase1_extract"
$trigger = New-ScheduledTaskTrigger -Monthly -DaysOfMonth 1 -At "02:00AM"
Register-ScheduledTask `
    -TaskName "CodeSage Phase1 Monthly Extract" `
    -Action $action -Trigger $trigger -RunLevel Highest

Phase1 Outcome:  A local folder (abap_files/) containing .abap source and .json metadata for every custom object in your SAP system (Z*/Y* namespace). Credentials are filtered, the RFC connection is read-only, and no data leaves your network. This folder is the shared input for Phase 2 (vector indexing) and Phase 3 (fine-tuning).

PHASE 2  —  Index & Embed

Phase 2 transforms the raw ABAP source files from Phase 1 into a searchable semantic vector database. 

Two steps run in sequence:

  • Step 1 — chunker.py:  Chunking: splits each ABAP file at logical boundaries (FORM/ENDFORM, METHOD/ENDMETHOD). One chunk = one function module, one method, or one report section. 150–500 tokens per chunk.
  • Step 2 — embedder.py:  Embedding: converts each chunk to a 768-number vector using the nomic-embed-text model running locally via Ollama. Stores vectors + original text in ChromaDB (4 collections).

The four ChromaDB collections:

Collection

Content

Chunk Unit

abap_programs

Executable reports and includes

One FORM routine or report body

function_modules

Function module source + parameters

One complete function module

abap_classes

ABAP OO classes, interfaces, methods

One METHOD block

documentation

SAP Help, Clean Core guidelines (optional)

One documentation paragraph

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

chunker.py — split ABAP source into logical segments

import pathlib, json, re
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Chunk:
    text: str; object_name: str; chunk_type: str; collection: str

FORM_RE      = re.compile(r'^FORM\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
METHOD_RE    = re.compile(r'^\s*METHOD\s+(\w+)', re.M | re.I)
ENDFORM_RE   = re.compile(r'^ENDFORM', re.M | re.I)
ENDMETHOD_RE = re.compile(r'^\s*ENDMETHOD', re.M | re.I)

def chunk_abap(abap_path, meta):
    src=abap_path.read_text(encoding='utf-8')
    lines = src.splitlines()
    name  = meta['name'];  typ = meta['type']
    chunks = []

    if typ == 'FM':                             # Function module = one chunk
        chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'FUNCTION', 'function_modules'))

    elif typ == 'CLAS':                         # Class = split on METHOD/ENDMETHOD
        buf, mname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = METHOD_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                mname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDMETHOD_RE.match(line) and mname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{mname}',
                                    'METHOD', 'abap_classes'))
                mname = None; buf = []
            elif mname:
                buf.append(line)
        if not chunks:
            chunks.append(Chunk(src, name, 'CLASS_DEF', 'abap_classes'))

    else:                                       # Program = split on FORM/ENDFORM
        buf, fname = [], None
        for line in lines:
            m = FORM_RE.match(line)
            if m:
                fname = m.group(1); buf = [line]
            elif ENDFORM_RE.match(line) and fname:
                buf.append(line)
                chunks.append(Chunk('\n'.join(buf), f'{name}.{fname}',
                                    'FORM', 'abap_programs'))
                fname = None; buf = []
            elif fname:
                buf.append(line)
        chunks.append(Chunk(src[:3000], name, 'BODY', 'abap_programs'))

    return [c for c in chunks if len(c.text.strip()) > 50]

if __name__ == '__main__':
    abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
    all_chunks = []
    for abap_file in abap_dir.glob('*.abap'):
        meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
        meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()                else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
        all_chunks.extend(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta))
    print(f'Total chunks: {len(all_chunks)}')

embedder.py — embed chunks and store in ChromaDB

import chromadb, requests, json, pathlib
from chunker import chunk_abap

db = chromadb.PersistentClient(path='./chromadb_store')

COLLECTIONS = {
    'abap_programs':    db.get_or_create_collection('abap_programs'),
    'function_modules': db.get_or_create_collection('function_modules'),
    'abap_classes':     db.get_or_create_collection('abap_classes'),
    'documentation':    db.get_or_create_collection('documentation'),
}

def embed(text):
    """Call local nomic-embed-text via Ollama. No cloud, no API key."""
    r = requests.post('http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings',
                      json={'model': 'nomic-embed-text', 'prompt': text})
    return r.json()['embedding']           # 768-dimensional vector

def store_chunk(chunk, idx):
    col = COLLECTIONS[chunk.collection]
    vec = embed(chunk.text[:2000])
    col.add(
        ids=[f'{chunk.object_name}_{idx}'],
        embeddings=[vec],
        documents=[chunk.text],
        metadatas=[{'object': chunk.object_name, 'type': chunk.chunk_type}])

abap_dir = pathlib.Path('../phase1_extract/abap_files')
for i, abap_file in enumerate(sorted(abap_dir.glob('*.abap'))):
    meta_file = abap_file.with_suffix('.json')
    meta = json.loads(meta_file.read_text()) if meta_file.exists()            else {'name': abap_file.stem, 'type': 'PROG'}
    for j, chunk in enumerate(chunk_abap(abap_file, meta)):
        store_chunk(chunk, j)
    if i % 100 == 0:
        print(f'  Indexed {i} files...')

print('ChromaDB indexing complete.')
for name, col in COLLECTIONS.items():
    print(f'  {name}: {col.count()} chunks')

Prerequisites & How to Run 

Step 1 — Activate venv and install dependencies

cd C:\codesage\phase2_index
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install chromadb requests
python -c "import chromadb; print('chromadb', chromadb.__version__)"

Step 2 — Install Ollama and download embedding model

# Install Ollama from ollama.com/download (Windows installer, free)
# After install, open a NEW PowerShell window:
ollama pull nomic-embed-text

ollama list
# NAME                    SIZE
# nomic-embed-text:latest 274 MB

Step 3 — Test Ollama is responding

# Save as test_ollama.py, run: python test_ollama.py
import requests
r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                  json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": "hello SAP"})
vec = r.json()["embedding"]
print(f"Embedding OK: {len(vec)} dimensions")
# Expected: Embedding OK: 768 dimensions

python test_ollama.py

Step 4 — Dry-run chunker to preview chunk count

python chunker.py
# Expected: Total chunks: 42,318  (varies by codebase size)

Step 5 — Run the embedder (main Phase 2 step, ~2 hours on CPU)

python embedder.py
#   Indexed 0 files...  Indexed 100 files...  ...
# ChromaDB indexing complete.
#   abap_programs:    18,420 chunks
#   function_modules: 12,305 chunks
#   abap_classes:      9,841 chunks

Step 6 — Test semantic search

# Save as test_search.py, run: python test_search.py
import chromadb, requests

def embed(text):
    r = requests.post("http://localhost:11434/api/embeddings",
                      json={"model": "nomic-embed-text", "prompt": text})
    return r.json()["embedding"]

db  = chromadb.PersistentClient(path="./chromadb_store")
col = db.get_collection("function_modules")
results = col.query(
    query_embeddings=[embed("validate vendor payment terms")],
    n_results=3)
for doc_text, meta in zip(results["documents"][0], results["metadatas"][0]):
    print(f"[{meta['object']}]  {doc_text[:80]}...")
# Expected top result: [Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS]  FUNCTION Z_VALIDATE...

python test_search.py

Phase2 Outcome:  ChromaDB running on-premise with 4 collections and tens of thousands of embedded ABAP chunks. Any developer query can be matched to the most relevant code in your system in under 100 ms — without sending a single line of code to the cloud. This vector index is used at runtime (Phase 4) to feed context into every LLM response. 

PHASE 3  —  Fine-Tune, Merge & Package for BYOM

Phase 3 is the heart of CodeSage. By the end of it, you have a version of LLaMA-3 that has learned your naming conventions, your preferred BAPIs, your error class patterns, and your ABAP coding style.

Artifacts:

Folder Structure

generate_qa.py — Step A: create training dataset via Claude

import anthropic, json, pathlib

client = anthropic.Anthropic()     # reads ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var

def generate_qa(chunk_text, object_name):
    msg = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-sonnet-4-6",
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content":
            f"You are an ABAP expert. Given this code from {object_name}, "
            f"write 4 developer questions whose answer is in this code.\n\n"
            f"CODE:\n{chunk_text}\n\nReturn a JSON list of strings."}])
    questions = json.loads(msg.content[0].text)
    return [{"prompt": q, "completion": chunk_text} for q in questions]

with open("training_data.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for path in pathlib.Path("../phase1_extract/abap_files").glob("*.abap"):
        chunk = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")[:2000]
        for pair in generate_qa(chunk, path.stem):
            f.write(json.dumps(pair) + "\n")
# Result: 30,000 - 50,000 lines in training_data.jsonl

train_qlora.py — Step B: QLoRA fine-tuning 

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer, TrainingArguments, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, TaskType
from trl import SFTTrainer
import torch

MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"

# Load base model in 4-bit NF4 (reduces GPU memory from ~28 GB to ~6-12 GB)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True)

model     = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID,
              quantization_config=bnb_config, device_map="auto")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)

# Attach LoRA adapters — only these tiny matrices will be trained
lora_config = LoraConfig(
    r=16, lora_alpha=32,
    target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"],
    lora_dropout=0.05, bias="none",
    task_type=TaskType.CAUSAL_LM)
model = get_peft_model(model, lora_config)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
# Output: trainable params: 13,631,488 (0.17% of 8B)

# Train
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model=model, tokenizer=tokenizer,
    train_dataset=dataset,
    dataset_text_field="text",
    max_seq_length=2048,
    args=TrainingArguments(
        output_dir="./lora-adapter",
        num_train_epochs=3,
        per_device_train_batch_size=4,
        gradient_accumulation_steps=4,
        learning_rate=2e-4,
        fp16=True, save_strategy="epoch", logging_steps=50))
trainer.train()
trainer.save_model("./lora-adapter")   # ~50 MB adapter file

merge_model.py — Step C: merge LoRA adapter into base model

from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer
from peft import PeftModel

base      = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct",
    torch_dtype="auto", device_map="cpu")
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct")

model  = PeftModel.from_pretrained(base, "./lora-adapter/")
merged = model.merge_and_unload()        # adapter deltas baked into base weights

merged.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./merged_model/")
print("Merge complete. ./merged_model/ (~16 GB) ready for Docker packaging.")

Dockerfile — Step 'D: vLLM container for SAP AI Core BYOM

FROM vllm/vllm-openai:latest
COPY ./merged_model/ /model/
EXPOSE 8080
CMD ["python", "-m", "vllm.entrypoints.openai.api_server", \
     "--model",             "/model", \
     "--port",              "8080", \
     "--served-model-name", "codesage-llama3", \
     "--max-model-len",     "8192", \
     "--dtype",             "bfloat16"]

serving-template.yaml — Step E: SAP AI Core BYOM registration

apiVersion: ai.sap.com/v1alpha1
kind: ServingTemplate
metadata:
  name: codesage-llama3
  labels:
    scenarios.ai.sap.com/id: codesage-scenario
    ai.sap.com/version: "1.0"
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
        - name: <your-registry-secret>
      containers:
        - name: kserve-container
          image: <your-registry>/codesage-llama3:v1
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              memory: "20Gi"
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"    # SAP AI Core allocates a managed GPU

Prerequisites & How to Run

Step A — Generate training data 

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install anthropic
$env:ANTHROPIC_API_KEY = "sk-ant-your-key-here"

python generate_qa.py
# Reads each .abap file, sends to Claude, saves (question, code) pairs

(Get-Content .\training_data.jsonl).Count
# Typical: 30,000 to 50,000 lines

Step B — Fine-tune: choose your GPU option

Option

GPU

Cost

RAM

Duration

Best For

Option A — Kaggle (recommended for first run)

NVIDIA T4 16 GB (free)

£0

16 GB GPU + 13 GB RAM

6–12 hrs

Anyone, no hardware needed

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU

RTX 3090 (24 GB) / RTX 4090 (24 GB) / A100 (40–80 GB)

Electricity only

24+ GB VRAM recommended

2–6 hrs

Faster iteration, larger batch, keep data on-prem

 Option A — Kaggle free GPU (T4 16 GB)

  1. Upload training_data.jsonl to Kaggle as a Dataset (kaggle.com → Datasets → New Dataset).
  2. Create a new Kaggle Notebook. In the right panel set Accelerator = GPU T4 x1 and enable Internet access.
  3. In the first notebook cell, install dependencies:

# Kaggle Cell 1 — install + HuggingFace login
!pip install -q transformers peft trl bitsandbytes accelerate datasets huggingface_hub
import huggingface_hub
huggingface_hub.login(token="hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN")

  1. Paste the contents of train_qlora.py into Cell 2 (update the dataset path to your Kaggle input path).
  2. Click Run All. Training takes 6–12 hours. You can close the browser — Kaggle keeps running.
  3. When complete, download the lora-adapter/ folder as ZIP. Extract to C:\codesage\phase3_finetune\lora-adapter\

Option B — Local NVIDIA GPU (RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100)

If you have a Windows machine with a modern NVIDIA GPU you can run the full training pipeline locally. This keeps all training data on-premise (no upload to Kaggle) and is typically 2–3× faster than a T4 due to higher memory bandwidth.

Additional prerequisites for local GPU training:

Step B2.1 — Verify NVIDIA setup in PowerShell

# Check GPU is recognised and driver version
nvidia-smi
# Expected output includes: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 / 4090 / A100
#   Driver Version: 535.xx  |  CUDA Version: 12.x

# Check CUDA toolkit
nvcc --version
# Expected: Cuda compilation tools, release 11.8 (or 12.x)

# If nvcc not found, CUDA Toolkit is not installed.
# Download from: https://developer.nvidia.com/cuda-downloads
# Choose: Windows -> x86_64 -> 11 -> exe (local)

Step B2.2 — Install Python dependencies for local GPU training

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Install PyTorch with CUDA support (match your CUDA version)
# For CUDA 11.8:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu118
# For CUDA 12.1:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cu121

# Verify GPU is visible to PyTorch
python -c "import torch; print('CUDA available:', torch.cuda.is_available()); print('GPU:', torch.cuda.get_device_name(0))"
# Expected: CUDA available: True  |  GPU: NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090

# Install training libraries
pip install transformers peft trl accelerate datasets huggingface_hub

# Install bitsandbytes (4-bit quantisation for QLoRA)
pip install bitsandbytes
# If bitsandbytes gives DLL errors on Windows, use the community Windows build:
pip install bitsandbytes --prefer-binary --extra-index-url=https://jllllll.github.io/bitsandbytes-windows-webui

Step B2.3 — Download LLaMA-3 base model weights

# Log in to HuggingFace (you must have accepted the Meta LLaMA-3 licence first)
# Visit: https://huggingface.co/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct and click Accept
python -c "import huggingface_hub; huggingface_hub.login(token='hf_YOUR_READ_TOKEN')"

# Download model (~16 GB, one-time)
python -c "
from huggingface_hub import snapshot_download
snapshot_download('meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct',
                  local_dir='C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model')
print('Download complete.')
"

# Check folder size (should be ~16 GB)
(Get-ChildItem -Path ".\base_model" -Recurse | Measure-Object -Property Length -Sum).Sum / 1GB

Step B2.4 — Update train_qlora.py to use the local model path

The only change needed from the Kaggle version is the MODEL_ID path — point it to the local download instead of HuggingFace Hub:

# In train_qlora.py, change this line:
MODEL_ID = "meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B-Instruct"     # Kaggle version (downloads from HF)

# To this for local GPU:
MODEL_ID = "C:\\codesage\\phase3_finetune\\base_model"   # uses local download

# You can also increase batch size on a 24 GB GPU for faster training:
# In TrainingArguments, change:
per_device_train_batch_size=4   # T4 safe value
# To:
per_device_train_batch_size=8   # RTX 3090/4090 with 24 GB VRAM

Step B2.5 — Run training locally

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

# Monitor GPU memory before starting
nvidia-smi

# Run training
python train_qlora.py

# In a second PowerShell window, monitor GPU usage while training runs:
while ($true) { nvidia-smi --query-gpu=utilization.gpu,memory.used,memory.total --format=csv,noheader; Start-Sleep 10 }
# Expected: gpu-util 95-100%,  memory.used ~14000 MiB / 24576 MiB (RTX 3090)

# Training completes in 2-6 hours (vs 6-12 on Kaggle T4)
# Adapter saved to: .\lora-adapter\

Step C — Merge adapter on Windows PC (~20 min, needs 32 GB RAM)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune
..\phase1_extract\codesage-env\Scripts\Activate.ps1

pip install transformers peft accelerate
python merge_model.py

# Verify output
Get-ChildItem .\merged_model
# Expected: config.json, tokenizer.json, model-00001-of-00004.safetensors, etc.

Step D — Build and push Docker image (Docker Desktop required)

cd C:\codesage\phase3_finetune

docker login   # enter Docker Hub credentials
docker build -t codesage-llama3:v1 .    # ~18 GB image, takes 10-20 min first time

docker tag  codesage-llama3:v1  your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1
docker push your-dockerhub-username/codesage-llama3:v1  # 30-60 min upload

Step E — Deploy to SAP AI Core (browser, SAP AI Launchpad)

  1. Open SAP AI Launchpad → ML Operations → Docker Registry Secrets → Add (your registry + credentials).
  2. ML Operations → Serving Templates → Upload YAML → select serving-template.yaml.
  3. ML Operations → Configurations → Create → select the codesage-llama3 template.
  4. ML Operations → Deployments → Create → select the configuration. Wait for status: RUNNING (5–10 min).
  5. Copy the Deployment URL and Deployment ID. Store both in BTP → Connectivity → Destinations as destination CODESAGE_AICORE.

Test the endpoint from PowerShell:

# test_aicore.py  -- python test_aicore.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("https://<token-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://<ai-core-url>/v2/inference/deployments/<deployment-id>/chat/completions",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "AI-Resource-Group": "default"},
    json={"model": "codesage-llama3",
          "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "What is ABAP?"}],
          "max_tokens": 200})
print(response.json()["choices"][0]["message"]["content"])
# Expected: ABAP stands for Advanced Business Application Programming...

Phase 3 Outcome:  A fine-tuned LLaMA-3 8B model — trained on your organisation's ABAP codebase using QLoRA — is running as a live, OpenAI-compatible inference endpoint on SAP AI Core. No local GPU is needed at runtime. Training cost: £0 on Kaggle free tier. The endpoint is reachable from BTP via a secure Destination. Phases 1, 2, and 3 are now all complete — Phase 4 wires them together.

PHASE 4  —  Runtime Query via SAP BTP

Phase 4 is the user-facing layer. It deploys a small Node.js service (BTP CAP) called the CodeSage Agent that orchestrates the full query pipeline:

Step

What Happens

Where

1

Developer types a plain English question in the UI or Teams bot

Browser / Teams

2

Agent embeds the question using nomic-embed-text (local Ollama)

On-premise

3

ChromaDB returns the top-5 most relevant ABAP code chunks

On-premise

4

Agent builds a structured prompt: context chunks + question

BTP CAP service

5

Prompt is sent to the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core BYOM

SAP AI Core

6

Answer returned in 2–5 s, citing real program names by name

Browser / Teams

 Artifacts:

Folder Structure

codesage-service.cds — service endpoint definition

// srv/codesage-service.cds
service CodeSageService @(requires: 'CodeSage.User') {

    // POST /codesage/query  { question, context_filter? }
    action query(
        question       : String not null,
        context_filter : String    // optional: 'function_modules' | 'abap_classes' etc.
    ) returns {
        answer      : String;
        sources     : array of { object_name: String; chunk_type: String; };
        model       : String;
        latency_ms  : Integer;
    };
}

query-handler.js — core orchestration: RAG retrieval + AI Core call

// srv/query-handler.js
const axios  = require('axios');
const xsenv  = require('@sap/xsenv');
const { retrieveChunks } = require('./chromadb-client');

const { destination } = xsenv.getServices({ destination: { tag: 'destination' } });

module.exports = (srv) => {
  srv.on('query', async (req) => {
    const { question, context_filter } = req.data;
    const t0 = Date.now();

    // 1. Retrieve top-5 relevant ABAP chunks from on-premise ChromaDB
    const collections = context_filter
      ? [context_filter]
      : ['abap_programs', 'function_modules', 'abap_classes'];
    const chunks  = await retrieveChunks(question, collections, 5);
    const context = chunks
      .map((c, i) => `[${i+1}] ${c.metadata.object} (${c.metadata.type}):\n${c.document}`)
      .join('\n\n');

    // 2. Build prompt
    const messages = [
      { role: 'system',
        content: 'You are CodeSage, an expert ABAP assistant. '
               + 'Answer ONLY using the retrieved context. '
               + 'Cite every object by name. End with a Sources section.' },
      { role: 'user',
        content: `RETRIEVED CONTEXT:\n${context}\n\nQUESTION: ${question}` }
    ];

    // 3. Call SAP AI Core BYOM (OpenAI-compatible endpoint)
    const token    = await getAICoreToken();
    const deployId = process.env.AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID;
    const response = await axios.post(
      `${destination.url}/v2/inference/deployments/${deployId}/chat/completions`,
      { model: 'codesage-llama3', messages, max_tokens: 1024, temperature: 0.1 },
      { headers: { Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`, 'AI-Resource-Group': 'default' } });

    const answer  = response.data.choices[0].message.content;
    const sources = chunks.map(c => ({ object_name: c.metadata.object,
                                       chunk_type:  c.metadata.type }));
    return { answer, sources, model: 'codesage-llama3', latency_ms: Date.now() - t0 };
  });
};

Prerequisites & How to Run

 Step 1 — Install developer tools (one-time)

# Verify Node.js after install
node --version    # Expected: v18.x.x+
npm  --version    # Expected: 9.x.x+

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk
cds --version     # Expected: @sap/cds: 7.x.x

npm install -g mbt
mbt --version     # Expected: 1.2.x

# Install cf CLI from github.com/cloudfoundry/cli/releases (Windows .exe installer)
cf --version      # Expected: cf version 8.x.x

Step 2 — Install Node.js dependencies and test locally

cd C:\codesage\phase4_runtime\codesage-agent
npm install

# Set local env vars for testing
$env:CHROMADB_URL           = "http://localhost:8000"
$env:AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  = "<your-deployment-id>"

cds watch
# [cds] - serving CodeSageService at http://localhost:4004/codesage

Test in a second PowerShell window while cds watch is running:

$body = @{ question = "How do we validate vendor payment terms?" } | ConvertTo-Json
Invoke-RestMethod -Method Post -Uri "http://localhost:4004/codesage/query" `
  -Headers @{ "Content-Type" = "application/json" } -Body $body
# Expected: answer + sources + latency_ms

Step 3 — Log in to BTP Cloud Foundry and create service instances

cf login -a https://api.cf.<your-region>.hana.ondemand.com
# Enter BTP email and password, select your org and space

cf create-service xsuaa        application  codesage-xsuaa        -c xs-security.json
cf create-service destination  lite         codesage-destination

cf services
# Both services should show: create succeeded

Step 4 — Build MTA archive and deploy to BTP

mbt build
# Creates: .\mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar

cf deploy mta_archives\codesage-agent_1.0.0.mtar
# Takes 3-5 minutes. Ends with:
# Application "codesage-agent" started at:
# https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com

Step 5 — Point deployed agent to on-premise ChromaDB

# Use SAP Cloud Connector to expose ChromaDB port to BTP, then:
cf set-env codesage-agent CHROMADB_URL           "https://virtual-chromadb-host:8000"
cf set-env codesage-agent AI_CORE_DEPLOYMENT_ID  "<your-deployment-id>"
cf restage codesage-agent

cf app codesage-agent
# Expected: requested state: started  instances: 1/1

Step 6 — End-to-end live test

# test_live.py  -- python test_live.py
import requests
token_resp = requests.post("<xsuaa-url>/oauth/token",
    data={"grant_type": "client_credentials"},
    auth=("<client-id>", "<client-secret>"))
token = token_resp.json()["access_token"]

response = requests.post(
    "https://codesage-agent-<id>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com/codesage/query",
    headers={"Authorization": f"Bearer {token}", "Content-Type": "application/json"},
    json={"question": "Do we have a function module for vendor payment term validation?"})
data = response.json()
print("Answer:",   data["answer"][:200])
print("Sources:",  data["sources"])
print("Latency:", data["latency_ms"], "ms")
# Expected:
# Answer: Yes — Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS validates vendor payment terms against T052...
# Sources: [{'object_name': 'Z_VALIDATE_VENDOR_PAYTERMS', 'chunk_type': 'FUNCTION'}]
# Latency: 2841 ms

Step 7 — Register as a Fiori Launchpad tile (optional)

  1. In BTP Cockpit → HTML5 Applications, upload the ui/webapp folder.
  2. In SAP Fiori Launchpad Configuration Cockpit, add a new tile pointing to the HTML5 app URL.
  3. Assign the tile to a role collection mapped to CodeSage.User. Developers see the tile when they open Fiori Launchpad.

Phase 4 Outcome:  A developer types a question in plain English and receives a contextualised ABAP answer in 2–5 seconds, citing real function modules and programs by name. Every query is authenticated via BTP XSUAA, grounded by ChromaDB retrieval, and generated by the fine-tuned LLaMA-3 on SAP AI Core. No source code leaves the SAP BTP perimeter. Developers stop reinventing — they reuse.

Summary — what the developer gets: Every CodeSage answer is a synthesis of three layers: (1) what your organisation has already built — reuse, do not reinvent; (2) what SAP recommends as the correct standard approach; (3) what is safe and compatible for the S/4HANA journey ahead. No other tool in the SAP ecosystem combines all three automatically in a single developer query response.

entry.title: Re: Can a rejected automatic retrofit be reprocessed again?
entry.content:
Hello, 

Once a transport categorized as automatic is rejected manually by a user the transport retrofit status changed to "Manually Retrofitted" and it cannot be retrofitted automatically any more, this manual rejection cannot be reverted.

Check the documentation in the Performing a Retrofit Help Portal content, pay attention to the Rejecting Retrofit Objects section as you could need to remove the created ToC manually.

"Please note that a ToC has been created when the source transport categorization is finished. This means that you have to remove the ToC manually from the import queue if you want to reject a retrofit."

Best regards,

Dolores

entry.title: TPM_Migration necessary for S4HANA conversion if Busness Function FIN_TRM_LR_FI_AN is not active?
entry.content:
My client upgraded to ECC6.0 EHP8 to do a S4HANA conversion (brownfield) afterward. Based on the simplification item check I receive the information that I need to do a migration of a money market produkt into the quantity ledger although I didn't activate the business function FIN_TRM_LR_FI_AN (2270529 - S4TWL - Mengenbuch bei Geldhandelsgeschäften immer aktiv - SAP for Me).

So my question is: do I need to execute the TPM_MIGRATION with conversion type 6 or is this not relevant for the conversion into the SAP S4HANA Private Cloud?
